card.intuition: Divide the array into a sorted prefix and an unsorted suffix. On each pass, find the minimum element in the unsorted suffix and swap it to the boundary — growing the sorted prefix by one element.
card.analogy: Picking cards from a shuffled deck: each round, scan the remaining cards, find the lowest, and place it at the front. After n rounds, the deck is sorted.
card.time_complexity: O(n²) — always, even for sorted input
when_to_use: When the cost of swapping is very high (selection sort does at most n-1 swaps)
Teaching the 'find minimum, place it' paradigm
gotchas: Not stable in the standard implementation — equal elements may be reordered by swapping a non-adjacent element
Always O(n²) comparisons — no early exit possible like bubble sort
Outperforms bubble sort on write-heavy systems due to O(n) swaps vs O(n²) swaps

exercise.prompt: Implement selection sort. Find the minimum in the unsorted region and swap it to position i.
hints: When arr[j] is smaller than arr[min_idx], update min_idx = j to track the new minimum's index
exercise.test_code:
assert selection_sort([64,25,12,22,11]) == [11,12,22,25,64]
assert selection_sort([1]) == [1]
assert selection_sort([]) == []
assert selection_sort([3,2,1]) == [1,2,3]
assert selection_sort([1,2,3]) == [1,2,3]
print('PASS')
